text:
E0 — Static Website + Deployment

Status: Working markdown version for Copilot context. 
Canonical source: Formal E0 specification document.

Build the public-facing single-page marketing website for Coaching Studio using Next.js App Router and deploy it through the existing Vercel setup.

This page is the public discovery layer for the platform. It must explain the value proposition, guide visitors to key sections, support web and mobile responsiveness, expose a persistent chatbot entry point, and be production-deployable on the agreed architecture.

Epic E0 covers:
Landing page
Navigation
Footer
Responsive behavior
SEO
Hosting
Domain behavior
Analytics
Routing
Error handling

This is a public marketing experience, not the authenticated app shell.

Assume T0, T1, T2, T3, and T4 are complete enough to support:
project setup
frontend architecture
Firebase/backend baseline
deployment pipeline
security and access-control foundation

Coaching Studio should be presented as a unified growth ecosystem for the coaching industry.

Core messaging:
One platform for coaching, assessments, programs, and transformation
Built for coaches, coaching companies, and individuals
Turn assessments into insight, insight into action, and action into measurable transformation
Programs, tools, events, and AI-supported reports in one connected experience

The page must:
communicate the platform value proposition clearly
support single-page anchored navigation
support responsive layouts across desktop, tablet, and mobile
include a persistent chatbot launcher
be SEO-ready
be analytics-ready
be deployable through the existing Vercel setup
follow the existing frontend and architecture conventions

Do not build the following in E0:
authentication flows
protected pages
app shell behavior
dashboards
admin console
program detail pages
tool execution flows
event registration flows
report generation flows
marketplace payments
full chatbot intelligence
CMS or admin content authoring tools

E0 includes only the chatbot launcher and placeholder modal/panel experience, not the real assistant intelligence.

The landing page should speak to:
Independent coaches
Coaching companies
Individual professionals / coachees
General visitors

Primary outcomes should be exploration, conversion, chatbot engagement, and eventual sign-in / registration.

The site should follow these principles:
Single-page, section-based storytelling
Fast-loading premium SaaS visual language
Clear value proposition above the fold
Mobile-first responsiveness
Sticky header and persistent chatbot launcher
Minimal friction to content discovery
Accessibility-first implementation
Externalized styling and behavior logic
No ad hoc inline implementation in page content files

The public page should be implemented as a single route with anchored sections in this order:
1. Header / Navigation
2. Hero
3. Platform overview
4. Benefits for coaches
5. Benefits for individuals
6. Assessment and transformation section
7. Programs section
8. Tools section
9. Events section
10. Impact counters / social proof
11. Sign In / Register CTA
12. Footer

Navigation items must be:
About
Programs
Tools
Events
Sign In / Register

Brand/logo on the left should return to #home.

Anchor mapping:
Coaching Studio → #home
About → #about
Programs → #programs
Tools → #tools
Events → #events
Sign In / Register → #auth

Behavior:
Sticky header
Smooth scroll
Active section indication
Mobile hamburger menu
Sign In / Register emphasized as CTA

Hero must include:
Brand/product name
One headline
One supporting paragraph
Two CTA buttons
One visual zone on desktop
Stacked layout on mobile

Recommended CTA labels:
Explore Programs
Sign In / Register

Hero copy should communicate:
programs
tools
events
AI-supported guidance
professional growth transformation

The page must include:
Hero headline and subheadline
Introductory description
Tagline or rotating statement area
Counters
Benefits for coaches
Benefits for individuals
Assessment and transformation explanation
Programs section
Tools section
Events section
Persistent chatbot launcher
Sign In / Register CTA section

Show four counters:
Coaches Supported
Professionals Benefited
Programs Available
Events Conducted

E0 should use static configurable seed values from a dedicated content/config file.

Recommended launch defaults:
Coaches Supported: 100+
Professionals Benefited: 1,000+
Programs Available: 25+
Events Conducted: 50+

Rules:
Labels must remain visible even if values fail
Counter animation is optional
Respect reduced-motion settings

Create separate sections for:
coaches
individuals / professionals

Coaches section should cover:
structured client journeys
programs and tools in one place
assessment-based insight
AI-supported reports and transformation planning
event-led engagement
professional credibility

Individuals section should cover:
guided self-discovery
access to programs and assessments
personalized growth insight
trackable transformation journey
events and continuous learning

Each audience section should use around 3 to 6 feature cards.

This section should clearly explain:
1. User completes an assessment
2. Platform interprets results
3. Results contribute to a transformation plan
4. Programs, tools, and events support progress over time

This is a key differentiator and should connect assessments with structured growth and AI-supported reporting.

A persistent chatbot launcher must remain visible throughout page scroll.

Requirements:
Fixed at bottom-right safe area
56 px desktop, 52 px mobile
Accessible label
Opens modal or side panel
Dismissible by close icon, Escape key, backdrop click, and explicit close action
Placeholder assistant content is acceptable in E0

Example placeholder:
greeting line
short assistant description
CTA for later continuation
optional contact fallback

Footer must include:
copyright line
privacy policy link
address
Instagram
Phone
WhatsApp
LinkedIn

Recommended additions:
email
small brand description
“All rights reserved”

Rules:
Footer content should come from centralized config
Missing optional links should be hideable safely
Do not hardcode repeated footer data across components

Supported breakpoints:
Mobile portrait: 360 to 767 px
Tablet: 768 to 1023 px
Desktop: 1024 px and above
Large desktop: 1440 px and above

Layout guidance:

Desktop padding 32 px

Mobile padding 16 px
Desktop 12-column behavior

All modals/popups must support:

close icon top-right
Escape key close
focus trap
background scroll lock
safe mobile width
accessible interaction patterns

Implement with Next.js metadata support:
unique page title
meta description
canonical URL
Open Graph title, description, URL, site name, locale, image
Twitter/X card metadata
robots directives
favicon and app icons

SEO/content rules:
one H1 only
semantic landmarks
clean anchor IDs and internal links
descriptive alt text
server-renderable primary marketing copy
optimized images
target strong Lighthouse scores

Core keyword themes:
coaching platform
coaching programs
coaching assessments
coaching tools
transformation plan
professional growth platform
coaching events

Hosting baseline:

production tied to main branch
preview deploys enabled for pull requests
environment separation follows dev / staging / prod setup
static assets should use Next.js / Vercel optimization

Domain rules:
support a canonical production domain
HTTPS / SSL active
standardize apex vs www
canonical metadata must match the chosen host
alternate host should redirect permanently
trailing slash behavior should be consistent

Capture at minimum:
page view
scroll depth: 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%
nav link clicks
hero CTA clicks
Sign In / Register CTA clicks
chatbot launcher click
chatbot modal open
chatbot modal close
footer social clicks

Recommended event names:
landing_page_view
landing_nav_click
landing_cta_click
landing_scroll_depth
chatbot_launcher_click
chatbot_modal_open
chatbot_modal_close
footer_social_click

Rules:

wrap analytics behind a small helper
avoid unnecessary PII collection on the public page

Routing rules:
primary page route is /
use fragment-based anchored sections
nav updates URL fragment without full reload
direct access to anchor URLs should land correctly
sticky header offset must be handled
unknown public routes use normal 404 behavior

Recommended implementation structure:
src/app/page.tsx
src/app/error.tsx
src/app/not-found.tsx
src/modules/marketing/
src/constants/site-content.ts
src/lib/analytics.ts
src/lib/seo.ts

E0 should handle gracefully:
missing decorative assets
analytics failure
chatbot placeholder failure
invalid external social links

Rules:
core content must remain usable if analytics fails
image failure must not break layout
chatbot failure should show fallback messaging
external links should be validated via config where possible
follow centralized frontend error utilities and app-level route boundaries

Use:
Next.js App Router
TypeScript
Tailwind
existing architecture conventions

Follow these rules:
no business logic in UI components
no direct Firestore reads from UI
keep config/content centralized
server-render main page shell where possible
use client components only for interactive behavior
keep hydration light
no secrets in frontend code
no privileged backend logic in frontend

``text
src/app/
 layout.tsx
 page.tsx
 error.tsx
 not-found.tsx

src/modules/marketing/
 components/
 header.tsx
 mobile-menu.tsx
 hero.tsx
 about.tsx
 benefits-coaches.tsx
 benefits-individuals.tsx
 transformation-flow.tsx
 programs.tsx
 tools.tsx
 events.tsx
 impact-counters.tsx
 cta-auth.tsx
 footer.tsx
 chatbot-launcher.tsx
 chatbot-modal.tsx
 hooks/
 use-active-section.ts
 use-scroll-depth.ts
 use-mobile-orientation.ts
 data/
 marketing-content.ts

src/lib/
 analytics.ts
 seo.ts

src/constants/

 site.ts
``

Public landing page exists at /
All required sections are present
Sticky header works
Persistent chatbot launcher works
Desktop, tablet, and mobile layouts work
Mobile navigation works
Footer content is complete
SEO metadata is implemented
Analytics events are wired
Anchor routing works correctly
Direct fragment loads land correctly
Graceful error behavior exists
Vercel deployment works through existing pipeline
No frontend secrets are exposed
No direct database calls from UI
Code is modular and maintainable

Delivered in current Coaching Studio scope:
Landing header keeps anchor-based navigation for Assessment Centre, Programs, and Events.
Header now has explicit logged-out and logged-in variants:
 - logged out: coach/learner toggle + Sign In/Register
 - logged in: initials dropdown menu replacing both toggle and Sign In/Register
Header logo is clickable and returns to Coaching Studio landing.
Mobile menu behavior mirrors desktop auth state behavior.

Cross-page continuity now aligned with this epic's navigation and public experience goals:

Sign In/Register remains visible only for unauthenticated users.

These may still need confirmation:
final brand colors and typography
final production domain
final footer legal entity details
final social/contact links
final static counter values
final chatbot modal vs side panel decision

Until finalized, use centralized config placeholders rather than hardcoded values.

E1 — App Skeleton + Mobile Wrapper

Status: Working markdown version for Copilot context. 
Canonical source: Formal E1 specification document.

Build the shared application skeleton for StudioVerse and prepare the platform for mobile-wrapper usage.

E1 establishes the base app shell that authenticated users will eventually work inside. It provides the common layout, placeholder screens, route structure, role-aware menu foundations, dashboard entry points, mobile navigation behavior, and Capacitor readiness.

Epic E1 covers:
shared app layout
placeholder pages
app routing structure
role-aware menu foundations
dashboard placeholders
mobile UI shell behavior
Capacitor integration baseline
mobile navigation
build readiness
environment configuration for app-shell usage

This epic is about the app framework, not full business feature delivery.

Assume T0, T1, T2, T3, and T4 are complete enough to support:
repo and environment setup
frontend architecture
Firebase baseline
deployment conventions
security and access-control baseline

E1 should build on the existing App Router structure and the shared StudioVerse architecture.

E1 creates the shared authenticated shell for the product.

It should ensure that:
the application has a stable layout foundation
major routes exist
placeholder screens are available
dashboard paths are ready
mobile navigation patterns are in place
the project is ready for Capacitor wrapping later
environment config supports the app shell correctly

E1 includes:
app shell layout
placeholder route pages
authenticated route structure
role-aware menu preparation
dashboard route placeholders
mobile shell adjustments
Capacitor baseline setup
mobile nav behavior
app-shell build validation
environment configuration alignment

Do not implement the following in E1:
final authentication flows
production user registration/login behavior
real dashboard widgets
business data integrations
program execution
tool execution
reports generation
admin workflows
full role enforcement logic beyond skeleton readiness
full mobile native feature implementation

E1 should prepare the structure for these features, not complete them.

E1 is the bridge between:
the public marketing site
and the future authenticated product experience

It should provide the structural base so later epics can plug into:
dashboards
auth
programs
tools
reports
cohorts
events
notifications
admin modules

The app shell should:
use shared layouts
support desktop and mobile behavior
provide a stable header/sidebar/navigation pattern
provide placeholder content regions
support future auth guards
support future role-aware navigation
be extendable without reworking the route foundation

The app should include:
root layout
authenticated app layout
stable header area
sidebar or equivalent desktop navigation
main content area
responsive mobile navigation behavior

General layout rules:
keep layout modular
separate shell components from business modules
preserve future role-driven menu behavior
avoid hardcoding business feature logic into the shell

Create placeholder pages for key routes so the structure exists before feature implementation.

These placeholders should:
render cleanly
identify the screen purpose
avoid broken navigation
be minimal but production-safe
make later feature replacement easy

Placeholder pages should exist for the main app areas that later epics depend on.

E1 should establish the authenticated route skeleton for areas such as:
dashboard
programs
tools
events
cohorts
assignments
reports
notifications
settings
admin

Rules:
route naming should align with the documented architecture
placeholder pages should compile cleanly
future guarded routes should fit naturally into the structure
avoid route patterns that will require later rework

Prepare the menu structure for role-aware rendering later.

This means:
define navigation groups
define menu labels
define route targets
prepare a clean structure for role filtering later
do not hardcode unstable ad hoc menu logic across multiple components

Roles and visibility logic do not need to be fully implemented yet, but the menu architecture should anticipate:
super admin
company
professional / coach
individual

Create dashboard entry routes and placeholder screens for the major user types.

At minimum, prepare for:
admin dashboard
company dashboard
professional / coach dashboard
individual dashboard

These can be placeholder shells for now, but the route structure should be ready for later dashboard implementation.

E1 should support responsive app-shell behavior beyond the public landing page.

Mobile expectations:
shell adapts to smaller screens
mobile nav is usable
touch targets are appropriate
sidebar behavior collapses or transforms cleanly
layout remains stable across common device sizes

This is not full native behavior yet, but it should be mobile-conscious and wrapper-friendly.

Prepare mobile navigation patterns such as:
menu trigger
drawer, sheet, or bottom navigation depending on current shell design
clean open/close behavior
accessible focus handling
predictable route access on mobile

Keep the mobile navigation implementation lightweight and extendable.

E1 should prepare the project for mobile wrapping with Capacitor.

This includes:
ensuring app-shell routing works cleanly in a wrapper-compatible way
confirming build output is compatible with the current approach
preparing the codebase for Capacitor config and later platform sync
avoiding web-only assumptions that would block mobile wrapping

This does not require full native feature integration in E1.

E1 should build cleanly with the app skeleton in place.

Validate:
no broken imports
no dead placeholder navigation
no route conflicts
no layout compilation failures
no environment config issues related to shell behavior

The app should remain deployable after adding the skeleton.

E1 should align the app shell with the environment model already established in the technical setup.

Environment behavior should:
support dev / staging / prod conventions
respect public vs server-side config boundaries
avoid exposing secrets
support the app-shell use case cleanly
remain consistent with the Firebase and Vercel architecture

Use:
Next.js App Router

shared module conventions

Follow these rules:

avoid direct backend logic inside placeholder UI
do not add unstable shortcuts that break later epic implementation
preserve the multi-tenant structure
keep role-aware behavior extensible
keep navigation config centralized where possible

Likely implementation areas include:

Dashboard header now follows the same top-nav language as View All pages.
Dashboard header includes clickable logo back to landing.
Dashboard top links route to View All pages for Assessment Centre, Programs, and Events.

Resulting shell consistency:

Header state and navigation expectations are now aligned with E1 shell consistency goals.

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text:
This document defines the business and functional requirements for the Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing capability in StudioVerse.

The purpose of this Epic is to enable the SuperAdmin to create, manage, and publish Programs for a specific tenant so that those Programs become visible to all Companies, Professionals, and Individuals within that tenant. This Epic establishes the canonical Program entity for StudioVerse and creates the foundation for future Program-related capabilities such as assignment, consumption, progress tracking, catalog commerce, promoted content handling, and creator-led publishing workflows.

This PRD is intended to provide a shared understanding across business, product, engineering, QA, and integration stakeholders so that all teams align on scope, behavior, data design, lifecycle rules, and forward compatibility.

---

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text: To understand the business purpose, tenant-level visibility model, governance approach, promoted-program readiness, and future scalability of Programs across StudioVerse.

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text: To define scope, lifecycle, ownership behavior, promoted flag behavior, acceptance criteria, dependencies, and future roadmap alignment.

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text: To implement the Program entity, create/edit/publish flows, validation rules, tenant scoping, promoted flag persistence, auditability, and future-compatible schema design.

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text: To derive test cases for Program creation, validation, visibility, lifecycle, tenant isolation, promoted-flag storage, and audit behavior.

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text: To understand how tenant-level Program catalogs will work for Coaching Studio, Training Studio, Recruitment Studio, and future StudioVerse verticals.

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text:
To ensure the Program schema and lifecycle are designed in a way that supports future integrations such as assignments, catalogs, marketplaces, notifications, analytics, promoted content placement, and approval workflows.

---

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text:
Programs are a foundational content object in StudioVerse. They represent structured offerings that can later be assigned, consumed, tracked, and monetized. This Epic introduces the first governed Program management capability by enabling the SuperAdmin to create Programs directly into a tenant-wide catalog.

A Program created by the SuperAdmin is always created for one specific tenant. Once published and active, it is visible to all Companies, Professionals, and Individuals within that tenant. This creates a controlled, curated, and scalable catalog layer for each tenant.

This Epic also introduces schema elements required for future growth. In later phases, Companies and Professionals will be able to create their own Programs. However, those creator-owned Programs will initially remain scoped to the Company or Professional boundary and will not automatically become visible tenant-wide. If broader visibility is desired, the creator will submit the Program to the SuperAdmin for publication review through a future workflow.

In addition, this Epic introduces a new Program-level attribute called promoted. This attribute indicates that the Program has been marked for differentiated handling in future product behavior. The exact business behavior of promoted Programs is intentionally out of scope for this Epic and will be defined later together with the publish-request and broader catalog promotion workflow. In this Epic, the requirement is to ensure the data model, validation layer, and admin management flow can store and manage this attribute without requiring future schema redesign.

---

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text:
StudioVerse is a multi-tenant platform serving different Studio contexts, such as Coaching Studio, Training Studio, and Recruitment Studio. Each tenant requires its own governed Program catalog so that learning and service-delivery assets can be curated and surfaced consistently across the tenant.

Programs are central to StudioVerse because they are expected to support:
tenant-level catalog browsing,
future assignment to Individuals and Cohorts,
future progress tracking,
future paid and free catalog offerings,
future analytics,
future promoted-program placement,
and future creator-led publishing models.

Without a stable Program model and SuperAdmin-driven catalog creation capability, later product layers would be forced to build on fragmented assumptions or require costly redesign.

---

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text:
StudioVerse currently needs a formal Program domain model and a controlled Program creation workflow.

The system must allow the SuperAdmin to create a Program for a selected tenant with the right metadata, lifecycle controls, availability windows, visibility behavior, and catalog-governance attributes. At the same time, the Program design must support future ownership models where Companies and Professionals can create Programs privately first and later request tenant-wide publication.

The model must also support a new promoted attribute so that future promotion-oriented behaviors can be built on top of the Program entity without schema changes.

Without this capability:
there is no canonical Program structure,
tenant-wide Program governance is missing,
future catalog and assignment work lacks a stable dependency,
future promotion workflows would require redesign,
and future publication workflows would require schema migration or restructuring.

---

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text:
The objectives of this Epic are:

Enable SuperAdmin to create Programs for a selected tenant.
Establish the canonical Program entity for StudioVerse.
Support all key Program metadata needed for listing, detail display, and lifecycle management.
Allow SuperAdmin-created Programs to become visible tenant-wide.
Ensure Program visibility follows status, publication, and date rules.
Support plain-text facilitator information without requiring internal user references.
Introduce and persist a promoted attribute on the Program entity.
Future-proof the schema for Company-owned and Professional-owned Programs.
Create the foundation for later publication request and approval workflows.
Create the foundation for later promoted-program behavior and surfacing rules.
Ensure auditability and governance of Program lifecycle actions.

---

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text:
The following are explicitly out of scope for this Epic:

Program modules and internal Program content authoring.
Program assignment to users or cohorts.
Program progress tracking and completion logic.
Program marketplace purchase and checkout.
Company self-publishing.
Professional self-publishing.
Publication approval workflow for creator-owned Programs.
Promoted-program display logic.
Promoted-program ranking, sorting, merchandising, or placement behavior.
Version management or content approval chains beyond basic audit logs.

---

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text:
This Epic includes the following capabilities:

SuperAdmin creates a Program.
SuperAdmin selects the target tenant.
SuperAdmin saves a Program as draft.
SuperAdmin publishes a Program to the tenant-wide catalog.
SuperAdmin edits Program metadata after creation.
Program supports thumbnail, video URL, credits, duration, details, dates, facilitator, and promoted flag.
Program supports operational status and publication state.
Program visibility is governed by tenant, publish state, and date rules.
Audit records are created for key Program actions.
Schema supports future creator-scoped Programs.
Schema supports future promoted-program workflows.

---

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text:
This Epic does not include:

Program module structure.
Rich learning path sequencing.
Assignment engine behavior.
Learner progress or module completion.
Marketplace monetization.
Request-to-publish workflow.
Approval queue for SuperAdmin review.
Notifications related to publication requests.
Promoted-program homepage placement, featured slots, or discovery rules.
Special pricing, ranking, or catalog treatment for promoted Programs.

---

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text: A tenant is the top-level Studio context under which users, content, and catalog visibility are organized. Examples include Coaching Studio, Training Studio, and Recruitment Studio.

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text: A Program is a tenant-scoped content offering that may later be browsed, assigned, consumed, tracked, and monetized.

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text:
Ownership scope defines who created the Program and the default governance boundary of the Program.

Supported ownership scopes:
platform
company
professional

This Epic implements platform creation only.

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text:
Catalog visibility defines who can discover the Program.

Supported visibility scopes:
tenant_wide
company_only
professional_only

This Epic implements tenant-wide visibility for SuperAdmin-created Programs.

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text:
Publication state represents catalog-governance readiness and supports future approval workflows.

Supported values:
draft
published
pending_publication_review
rejected_publication

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text:
Status represents the operational lifecycle of the Program.

Supported values:
draft
published
inactive
archived

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text:
promoted is a Program-level attribute that indicates the Program has been marked for differentiated treatment in future catalog and publishing workflows.

In this Epic:
promoted is stored as metadata only,
it does not change visibility rules by itself,
it does not automatically publish a Program,
it does not automatically affect ranking or display,
and it does not override tenant, publication, or lifecycle rules.

The functional meaning of promoted Programs will be defined in a future Epic together with the publish-request workflow.

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text: The SuperAdmin is the only actor in scope for this Epic who can create and manage Programs in the tenant-wide catalog.

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text: Out of scope for creation in this Epic, but included in future schema design. Company-created Programs will later be supported with narrower visibility by default.

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text: Out of scope for creation in this Epic, but included in future schema design. Professional-created Programs will later be supported with narrower visibility by default.

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing

Not a creating actor in this Epic. Individuals are consumers of tenant-visible Programs.

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text:
The system shall allow the SuperAdmin to create a Program for a selected tenant.

Program name

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text:

No system-level user reference, relational link, or internal actor lookup is required for facilitator in this Epic.

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text:

ownershipScope = platform

company-created Programs

epicTitle: Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing
text:
Programs created by SuperAdmin shall default to tenant-wide catalog visibility when published.

all Companies in the tenant,
all Professionals in the tenant,
all Individuals in the tenant.

epicTitle: Event Management and Tenant Event Publishing
text:
This document defines the business and functional requirements for Event Management and Tenant Event Publishing in StudioVerse.

The purpose of this Epic is to enable the SuperAdmin to create, manage, publish, and promote Events for a specific tenant so that those Events become visible to all Companies, Professionals, and Individuals within that tenant, and selected Events can be elevated on the tenant landing page.

This Epic establishes the canonical Event entity for StudioVerse and creates the foundation for future Event-related capabilities such as registration, reminders, ticketing, referrals, attendance, creator-led publishing workflows, and promoted landing-page placement.

This PRD is intended to provide a shared understanding across Business, Product, Engineering, QA, and Integrations teams so that all stakeholders align on scope, lifecycle behavior, data design, tenant visibility, promotion behavior, and future extensibility.

---

epicTitle: Event Management and Tenant Event Publishing
text:
Events are the community layer of StudioVerse and are intended to support webinars, workshops, masterclasses, cohort sessions, and other live or scheduled engagements across tenants.

This Epic introduces the first governed Event management capability by enabling the SuperAdmin to create Events directly into a tenant-wide catalog from the SuperAdmin page. A SuperAdmin-created Event is always created for one specific tenant and, once published and active, is visible to all Companies, Professionals, and Individuals within that tenant.

This Epic also introduces a promoted attribute for Events. Promotion is not the same as publication. Publication determines whether an Event is visible in the tenant catalog. Promotion determines whether an already eligible Event is elevated and shown on the tenant landing page above other Events.

In a later phase, Companies and Professionals will be able to create their own Events, but those Events will not automatically become tenant-wide. Instead, they will require publication by the SuperAdmin through a future phase-2 workflow. That workflow is out of scope for this Epic, but the Event model defined here must support it from the start so that no schema redesign is required later.

---

epicTitle: Event Management and Tenant Event Publishing
text:
StudioVerse is a multi-tenant platform where each tenant requires its own governed Event catalog. Events are strategically important because they create the community heartbeat of a tenant and provide a structured way to deliver webinars, workshops, masterclasses, live sessions, and future paid engagements.

Events are expected to support:
tenant-level event discovery,
community engagement,
live learning and interaction,
future reminder and notification workflows,
future RSVP and attendance workflows,
future event sharing and referral behavior,
future ticketing and monetization,
and curated promotion on tenant landing pages.

Without a stable Event model and governed creation flow, later Event features would be forced to build on unstable assumptions and would likely require rework.

---

epicTitle: Event Management and Tenant Event Publishing
text:
StudioVerse requires a formal Event domain model and a controlled Event creation workflow so that the SuperAdmin can create Events for a selected tenant with the correct metadata, schedule, location address/city, lifecycle state, visibility behavior, and promotion behavior.

The system must also support future ownership models where Companies and Professionals can create their own Events, but those Events must be published by the SuperAdmin in phase 2 before receiving broader tenant visibility.

Without this capability:
there is no canonical Event structure,
tenant-wide Event governance is missing,
there is no clear separation between visibility and promotion,
future reminder, RSVP, ticketing, and attendance workflows lack a stable dependency,
and future creator-led publishing workflows would require schema migration or redesign.

Create the foundation for later Event publication approval workflows.
Create the foundation for later RSVP, reminders, sharing, attendance, and ticketing workflows.
Ensure auditability and governance of Event lifecycle actions.

This document defines the business and functional requirements for the Tools module in StudioVerse, with primary focus on the Assessment Engine.

The purpose of this Epic is to enable StudioVerse to create, manage, publish, assign, deliver, score, and analyze structured Assessments across Coaching Studio, Training Studio, and Recruitment Studio using a reusable and scalable engine instead of one-off custom implementations.

This Epic establishes the canonical Assessment model for StudioVerse and provides the foundation for AI-driven narrative reporting, embeddable assessment delivery, future question-generation workflows, longitudinal data capture, and gamified assessment experiences.

This PRD is intended to serve as the functional source of truth for product, engineering, QA, design, and implementation teams, and may also be used as an input specification for AI coding tools such as GitHub Copilot.

---

This document explains how the Assessment Engine creates strategic platform value by turning professional knowledge into reusable digital assets that generate insights, reports, and engagement data at scale.
This document defines the scope, terminology, metadata, lifecycle, render-style behavior, AI analysis behavior, assignment alignment, and future extensibility of Assessments.

This document provides the functional requirements needed to implement the Assessment Engine using reusable renderers, metadata-driven runtime behavior, backend-controlled business logic, and AI report generation through server-side integrations.
This document provides the basis for validating Assessment creation, question-bank association, runtime rendering, answer capture, scoring, AI analysis, report generation, and visibility behavior.
This document describes the supported Assessment interaction patterns and the expected reusable UX treatment of different assessment render styles.

This document ensures that Assessments integrate cleanly with assignments, widgets, notifications, reports, analytics, PDF generation, and future publishing flows.

---

The Tools module is one of the most strategically differentiated parts of StudioVerse because it does more than display content. It captures structured participant response data, interprets that data, and turns it into meaningful professional insight through AI-generated analysis and reports.

Within this module, the official term will be Assessment rather than Test.

An Assessment is a structured, purpose-driven instrument used to evaluate a participant’s awareness, capabilities, style, readiness, preferences, strengths, or developmental profile in a specific context.

This Epic introduces a reusable Assessment Engine in which multiple Assessments can be created from common patterns. Although StudioVerse may host 12 to 20 Assessments in the initial wave, those Assessments will be delivered through a small number of standard render styles rather than one custom page per Assessment.

Each Assessment will have its own identity, context, participant benefit, AI analysis prompt, question bank, and delivery configuration. At runtime, the platform will fetch the appropriate number of questions for that Assessment, select the correct renderer based on metadata, capture participant answers, and send the structured output to an AI model such as Groq or Gemini for narrative report generation.

---

Across coaching, training, and recruitment use cases, Assessments are not simply quizzes. They are structured mechanisms for understanding a participant more deeply, identifying developmental patterns, and guiding programs and interventions more effectively.

In Coaching Studio in particular, the foundational direction for this capability comes from leadership-oriented Assessments previously used in thecoachdinesh.com to help evaluate skills, capabilities, blind spots, and leadership potential.

StudioVerse now requires a scalable platform-native version of that capability. The platform must support many Assessments, but it should not implement each one as a separate custom product artifact. Instead, Assessments must run on a shared engine so that authoring, delivery, scoring, AI reporting, and future analytics can scale efficiently.

---

StudioVerse currently needs a formal Assessment domain model and a controlled Assessment Engine architecture so that the platform can define Assessment metadata, store question banks, render different interaction styles, capture participant answers, and generate AI-driven reports.

The platform must also support different styles of Assessment delivery. Some Assessments will behave like standard single-choice quizzes, while others will include instant feedback, image-based questions, selection-transfer interactions, and future gamified experiences.

Without this capability:
there is no canonical Assessment structure,
Assessment experiences would be hardcoded one by one,
AI reporting would lack Assessment-specific prompts and context,
question storage and reuse would be inconsistent,
and the platform would not have a scalable foundation for future Assessment growth.

---

The objectives of this Epic are:

Establish Assessment as the canonical terminology and entity in the Tools module.
Enable SuperAdmin to create and manage Assessments for a tenant.
Support Assessment metadata such as short description, long description, context, and participant benefit.
Support multiple reusable Assessment render styles.
Store an Assessment-specific AI analysis prompt for result interpretation.
Optionally store an Assessment-specific AI question-generation prompt for authoring support.
Store question banks against an Assessment rather than hardcoding questions into page components.
Allow the platform to fetch only the configured number of questions for a given Assessment attempt.
Support AI-generated narrative reports through Groq, Gemini, or equivalent backend AI services.
Integrate Assessments with assignment workflows, report delivery, and future widget-based usage.
Future-proof the module for richer gamification and creator-led Assessment authoring.

---

The following are explicitly out of scope for this Epic:

Creating 12 to 20 custom-coded pages for each individual Assessment
Fully dynamic live question generation during participant runtime
Advanced psychometric calibration and norming
Adaptive testing that changes difficulty based on prior answers
Assessment marketplace pricing and monetization rules
Enterprise benchmarking dashboards
Full multilingual localization in the first release
Real-time multiplayer assessment experiences
Fully autonomous, unreviewed AI-generated question delivery at runtime

---

This Epic includes the following capabilities:

SuperAdmin creates and manages Assessment definitions
Assessment metadata is stored in the datastore
Questions are associated with an Assessment through Assessment ID
Assessment-specific AI analysis prompts are stored
Optional Assessment-specific AI question-generation prompts are stored
Runtime fetches the configured number of questions for an Assessment attempt
Assessment delivery is driven by metadata and render style
The frontend uses reusable render-style layouts or components instead of one custom page per Assessment
Participant responses are captured and stored
Structured result data is sent to backend AI analysis services
AI-generated report output is stored and made available to users
Assessment completion can integrate with assignment state transitions

---

This Epic does not include:

live collaborative assessment authoring by multiple admins,
question marketplace and cross-tenant sharing,
advanced adaptive branching engines,
fully autonomous AI-generated question sets shown without review,
deep psychometric scoring models,
and multiplayer gamified assessment formats.

---
A Tool is a functional interactive resource within StudioVerse that allows a user to provide input and receive a structured result, outcome, or insight.

An Assessment is a type of Tool that presents a structured set of questions or interactive tasks to a participant in order to evaluate awareness, capability, preferences, behavior, readiness, or other development-related dimensions.
A Question Bank is the collection of approved questions associated with a specific Assessment and stored against that Assessment for runtime selection.
An Assessment Attempt is a single participant interaction session in which the platform fetches questions, records answers, calculates raw outputs, and generates or requests a final report.
An AI Analysis Prompt is the Assessment-specific instruction template used by the backend AI service to interpret submitted answers and generate a meaningful narrative report.
An AI Question Generation Prompt is an optional Assessment-specific instruction template used during admin authoring to generate draft question suggestions for review and storage.

Render Style defines how the Assessment is visually presented and how the participant interacts with the Assessment on the screen.

---
The SuperAdmin can create, edit, configure, publish, and govern Assessments and their associated Question Banks across the tenant.

A Professional can assign Assessments, review outputs, and use reports to guide sessions or interventions. In future phases, Professionals may also create their own Assessments subject to governance rules.

An Individual is the participant who takes the Assessment and receives the resulting report or insight output.

---

The system shall allow the SuperAdmin to create an Assessment for a selected tenant.

The Assessment creation flow shall support capture of:
Assessment name
Assessment short description
Assessment long description
Assessment context
Assessment benefit
Assessment type
Render style
Number of questions in bank
Number of questions to present per attempt
AI analysis prompt
Optional AI question-generation prompt
Status
Publication state
Ownership metadata
The platform shall use the term Assessment in product language, documentation, admin forms, participant-facing screens, and system metadata rather than the term Test.

Each Assessment shall support richer descriptive metadata so that both the participant experience and the AI interpretation remain grounded in context and purpose rather than only raw scores.

At minimum, the system shall store:
short description,
long description,
Assessment context,
and participant benefit.
Each Assessment shall support an associated Question Bank stored in the datastore and linked by assessmentId rather than hardcoded into page components.

The system shall allow each Assessment to define:
total question count available in the bank,
and the number of questions to be fetched and presented for a participant attempt.

The system shall not require all stored questions to be displayed in every attempt.

At runtime, the system shall:
load Assessment metadata,
fetch the configured set of questions,
choose the correct render style,
display the questions using the appropriate reusable renderer,
collect participant responses,
and submit the result payload for scoring and AI interpretation.
Each Assessment shall store an Assessment-specific AI analysis prompt used after submission to interpret the participant’s responses and generate the report.
The system should optionally support a separate AI prompt for generating draft questions during Assessment authoring so that question creation and answer interpretation remain separate concerns.

The frontend shall use a reusable renderer model rather than a separate custom page for every Assessment.

This means the platform shall maintain a small number of reusable render-style pages or components and use metadata to decide which one to render for a given Assessment.

After an Assessment Attempt is completed, the system shall:
store the participant’s submitted answers,
produce raw result data,
send structured Assessment context plus answers to the configured AI provider,
receive AI analysis,
create a report record,
and make the report available to the appropriate users.

---
The first release of the Assessment Engine shall support the following render styles.
A participant sees a question and selects one answer from multiple radio-button options.
A participant sees multiple options and receives immediate green or red feedback when selecting an answer, creating a more interactive and game-like experience.
A participant sees a list of items on one side and selects items which move to another area, visually representing chosen responses.
A participant sees an image, supporting descriptive text or scenario text, and then answers a single-choice question based on that visual context.

The platform should support a fifth, more gamified style. The preferred candidate is a drag-and-drop matching or ordering interaction because it is interactive, intuitive, and still manageable in an MVP architecture.

The engine shall be designed so that future render styles can be added without redesigning the whole Assessment model.

---

The Assessment entity shall support at minimum the following fields:

id
tenantId
name
shortDescription
longDescription
assessmentContext
assessmentBenefit
assessmentType
renderStyle
questionBankCount
questionsPerAttempt
analysisPrompt
questionGenerationPrompt
status
publicationState
ownershipScope
ownerEntityId
createdBy
updatedBy
createdAt
updatedAt
publishedAt

The Assessment Question entity shall support at minimum:

id
assessmentId
tenantId
questionText
questionType
renderStyle
options
correctAnswer
scoringRule
imageUrl
imageDescription
displayOrder
weight
tags
isActive
createdAt
updatedAt

The Assessment Attempt entity shall support at minimum:

id
assessmentId
tenantId
userId
assignmentId
questionsServed
answersSubmitted
rawScore
rawResultPayload
status
startedAt
completedAt

The Assessment Report entity shall support at minimum:

id
assessmentId
attemptId
tenantId
userId
aiProvider
analysisPromptUsed
aiResponseRaw
reportSummary
reportStructuredData
pdfUrl
createdAt

Implementation may begin with separate collections for:
assessments
assessmentQuestions
assessmentAttempts
assessmentReports

This is preferred over hardcoding questions in UI code or embedding large question sets directly into route-level components.

---

The platform shall use Assessment as the official term for this capability.
An Assessment must belong to one tenant.
Questions must be associated with an Assessment by assessmentId.
Questions must not be hardcoded into 12 to 20 individual frontend pages.
The number of questions shown in an attempt may be fewer than the number stored in the Question Bank.
AI analysis prompts must be stored per Assessment, not as one global prompt for all Assessments.
AI question-generation prompts and AI analysis prompts should be treated as separate concerns.
Runtime Assessment behavior must be chosen from metadata rather than custom code per Assessment.
External AI model calls must happen from backend services only, not from the frontend.
Assessment completion should support assignment completion workflows when the Assessment was assigned through the platform.

---

The platform should not create separate page.tsx files for every Assessment. That approach may seem fast initially but will create duplication, inconsistency, and high maintenance overhead as the number of Assessments grows.

Instead, the recommended architecture is:
one generic Assessment runtime route,
a small set of reusable renderer components or pages,
metadata-driven renderer selection,
separate Assessment and Question storage,
and backend-driven scoring and AI analysis.

This approach is more scalable, more consistent, easier to test, and better aligned with StudioVerse’s configuration-driven product philosophy.

---

Each Assessment shall carry a dedicated AI analysis prompt because the meaning of the participant’s answers depends on the specific purpose of the Assessment.

For example, an Assessment focused on self-awareness requires a different interpretive lens than an Assessment focused on decision-making, leadership style, blind spots, or developmental readiness.

Therefore, the system must send not only answers and scores, but also the Assessment context, benefit, and prompt instructions when generating the final report.

The platform should also support provider abstraction so that report generation may be performed by Groq, Gemini, or equivalent approved AI services without changing the Assessment model.

---

As a developer, 
I want a canonical Assessment model, 
so that all Assessments use one stable structure.

Acceptance Criteria
Assessment terminology is used consistently.
Metadata fields include short description, long description, context, and benefit.
Assessment supports render style and AI prompt fields.
Schema supports linked Question Banks and reports.

---

As a SuperAdmin, 
I want to create an Assessment definition, 
so that the platform can deliver it to users.

Acceptance Criteria
SuperAdmin can create a draft Assessment.
Required metadata can be stored.
AI analysis prompt can be stored.
Assessment belongs to the selected tenant.

---

sectionTitle: SuperAdmin Can Associate Questions to an Assessment
text:
As a SuperAdmin, 
I want to add questions to an Assessment, 
so that the Assessment can be delivered dynamically.

Acceptance Criteria
Questions are stored against assessmentId.
Questions are not embedded in UI code.
Questions can support different interaction styles.

---

sectionTitle: Runtime Chooses the Correct Render Style
text:
As a participant, 
I want the Assessment to display correctly based on its interaction pattern, 
so that the experience feels intuitive.

Acceptance Criteria
Runtime reads render-style metadata.
Correct renderer is selected.
The same renderer can be reused across multiple Assessments.

---

sectionTitle: Platform Fetches Configured Number of Questions
text:
As a Product Owner, 
I want each Assessment to control how many questions appear per attempt, 
so that the platform can support both fixed and subset-driven delivery.

Acceptance Criteria
Assessment stores questionsPerAttempt.
Runtime fetches only the configured number.
The Question Bank can contain more questions than the attempt displays.

---

As a Professional, 
I want submitted Assessment answers to be analyzed in the right context, 
so that the resulting report is meaningful and useful.

Acceptance Criteria
Assessment-specific AI prompt is used.
Structured answers are sent to backend AI services.
AI response is stored with the report record.

---

As a Professional, 
I want assigned Assessments to update completion status when finished, 
so that assignment tracking remains accurate.

Acceptance Criteria
If an Assessment is assignment-linked, completion updates assignment state.
Report generation can happen after submission.
Appropriate users can access the output.

---

QA should validate the following scenarios:

Assessment creation with all required metadata
Correct storage of short description, long description, context, and benefit
Question association by assessmentId
Correct fetch count for questionsPerAttempt
Correct renderer selection based on render-style metadata
Single-choice flow behavior
Instant-feedback flow behavior
Select-and-move flow behavior
Image-based flow behavior
Gamified flow behavior where supported
Answer persistence
Backend AI prompt selection
Report creation
Assignment completion updates
Tenant isolation across all Assessment records

---

This Epic is expected to integrate with:
the Assignments engine for delivery and completion tracking,
the Reports engine for AI-generated outputs and PDFs,
Notifications for report delivery,
Widgets for embeddable external Assessment entry points,
and future analytics services for longitudinal insight tracking.

The implementation should ensure that all AI calls occur in backend services or functions, not in the frontend.

---

This Epic depends on:
authenticated platform access,
tenant-aware authorization,
datastore persistence,
backend service or function layer for business logic,
AI provider integration,
report-generation capability,
and assignment/report module interoperability.

This Epic may later be extended by:
Assessment Widget Publishing Epic
Assessment Marketplace Epic
Professional-Created Assessment Epic
Assessment Benchmarking and Analytics Epic
Advanced Gamification Epic

---

If each Assessment is custom-built as a separate page, maintenance cost will rise quickly and the engine will not scale.
If AI prompts are global rather than Assessment-specific, report quality will decline because the analysis will ignore Assessment context.
If question-generation prompts and analysis prompts are not separated, the platform will mix authoring concerns with interpretation concerns.
If questions are generated live at runtime without prior review, content quality and reliability may become inconsistent.
If render styles are not modeled explicitly, frontend complexity will increase and reuse will decrease.

---

This Epic is considered complete when:

Assessment is implemented as the canonical Tools terminology.
SuperAdmin can create and manage Assessments.
Assessment metadata includes short description, long description, context, and benefit.
Questions are stored separately and linked to the Assessment.
Runtime fetches the configured number of questions.
Assessments render correctly through reusable style-based components.
Assessment-specific AI prompts are stored and used.
Completed Attempts generate reports through backend AI workflows.
Assignment-linked Assessments update assignment status appropriately.
Product, Engineering, and QA approve the implementation behavior.

---

This document may be used as an implementation specification for GitHub Copilot or other AI coding tools.

When using this document as a coding reference:
treat this file as the source of truth for Assessment terminology and structure,
do not create one custom page per Assessment,
implement a metadata-driven Assessment runtime,
store Questions separately from renderer code,
keep AI question generation and AI analysis as separate concerns,
and ensure all AI model invocation is done through backend services or functions.

This document defines the business and functional requirements for the Manage Wallet capability in StudioVerse.

The purpose of this Epic is to allow the SuperAdmin to issue platform coins to tenant entities and end users, and allow recipients to see their available balance within My Wallet after login.

This Epic also establishes the administrative controls, wallet visibility model, transaction tracking, dashboard summary behavior, and future-ready ledger structure required for controlled coin-based access and usage across the StudioVerse platform.

---
This document explains how coins act as a governed internal value mechanism that can be issued centrally, distributed to different user types, and later consumed through platform activity.
This document defines the scope, terminology, role-based behavior, wallet data model, issuance flow, dashboard summary rules, and future extensibility for Manage Wallet.

This document provides the functional requirements needed to implement wallet balances, coin issuance workflows, transaction ledgers, admin controls, and dashboard summary metrics in a scalable multi-tenant structure.
This document provides the basis for validating coin issuance, role-based assignment, wallet visibility, dashboard totals, unused vs utilized calculations, and tenant-level correctness.

This document describes the user-facing wallet experiences, SuperAdmin issuance flow, and dashboard entry points that must be intuitive and role-aware.

---

StudioVerse requires a platform-native mechanism to issue and track coins that can be assigned by the SuperAdmin to Companies, Coaches / Professionals, or Individuals.

Once coins are issued, the logged-in recipient must be able to see their balance in My Wallet, creating a direct and transparent connection between administrative allocation and user-level visibility.

The SuperAdmin must also have a dedicated management entry point called Manage Wallet, where wallets can be reviewed and coins can be assigned after selecting the tenant and the target user type.

In addition, the SuperAdmin dashboard must contain a summary tile that shows overall platform utilization in a form such as 10/20, where:

10 = total utilized coins
20 = total issued coins across all tenants

Selecting that tile must take the SuperAdmin directly to the Manage Wallet page.

---

As StudioVerse evolves into a structured multi-tenant platform with centrally governed experiences, it requires an internal allocation model that allows the platform owner to control usage and entitlement in a measurable way.

Coins provide that mechanism. They can represent access value, usage credits, or controlled platform entitlement units that the SuperAdmin can allocate across the ecosystem.

This model is especially valuable in a multi-tenant setup because allocation may need to happen at more than one level:

to a Company / Tenant,
to a Coach / Professional,
or directly to an Individual user.

---

StudioVerse currently needs a structured Wallet capability that allows coins to be issued, surfaced to users, and tracked consistently across the platform.

Without this capability:

there is no canonical place to assign coins,
users cannot clearly see what has been allocated to them,
administrators cannot track how many coins have been issued or used,
and the platform lacks the ledger foundation needed for future controlled consumption use cases.

The platform also requires a clear separation between:

coin issuance,
coin visibility, and
coin utilization,

so that the system can accurately report assigned balance versus actual usage over time.

---

The objectives of this Epic are:

Enable SuperAdmin to assign coins to a Company, Coach / Professional, or Individual.
Allow logged-in users to see their coin balance in My Wallet.
Add a dedicated Manage Wallet menu item to the SuperAdmin navigation.
Allow the SuperAdmin to choose a tenant before assigning coins.
Allow the SuperAdmin to choose a target user type before assigning coins.
Record every allocation in a transaction-safe ledger structure.
Track total issued coins across all tenants.
Track utilized coins separately from issued coins.
Show a dashboard tile summarizing utilized vs issued totals in a format such as 10/20.
Allow the dashboard tile to deep-link to the Manage Wallet page.
Future-proof the system for controlled debit / consumption scenarios.

---

The following are out of scope for this Epic:

external payment gateway integration,
purchase of coins by end customers,
invoicing and taxation,
refund workflows,
exchange rates or real-money conversion,
promotional coupon campaigns,
coin transfer between users,
and advanced finance reporting.

This Epic is focused on internal administrative issuance and wallet visibility, not public commerce.

---

This Epic includes the following capabilities:

SuperAdmin can assign coins.
Coin assignment can target Company / Tenant, Coach / Professional, or Individual.
Target entity selection is tenant-aware.
Wallet balances are visible to the logged-in user in My Wallet.
SuperAdmin dashboard shows utilized vs issued counts.
Dashboard tile navigates to Manage Coins.
All issuance records are stored in a wallet ledger or transaction model.
The system supports unused balance calculation.
The system is structured for future debit / consumption logic.

---

This Epic does not include:

direct end-user coin purchases,
peer-to-peer transfers,
subscription billing tied to coins,
bulk CSV import of coin assignments,
accounting exports,
and marketplace commerce logic.

---
A Coin is an internal StudioVerse value unit issued by the platform for controlled entitlement, balance tracking, and future usage consumption.
A Wallet is the system-managed balance container through which a logged-in user or entity can view issued, used, and available coin values.
Coin Issuance is the action by which the SuperAdmin allocates a specified number of coins to a valid target entity.
A Wallet Transaction is a ledger record representing a balance-affecting event such as issue, adjustment, debit, reversal, or expiry.
Utilized Coins are coins that have already been consumed by a valid platform action.

The Target User Type defines who receives the coin allocation:
Company / Tenant
Coach / Professional
Individual

---
The SuperAdmin can issue coins, review allocation data, and track issued versus utilized totals across the platform.
A Company may receive coins through the SuperAdmin allocation flow and may later use those coins for platform-controlled activity.
A Coach / Professional may receive coins directly through the SuperAdmin flow and must be able to view them in My Wallet once logged in.

An Individual may receive coins directly and must be able to see the assigned balance in My Wallet after login.

---

The SuperAdmin dashboard shall contain a tile showing:
total utilized coins,
total issued coins,
and a compact ratio-style summary such as 10/20.

The tile shall be clickable and shall navigate the user to the Manage Wallet page.

The system shall provide a SuperAdmin-only page for wallet management.

The page shall support:
existing wallet listing,
user-type radio filtering,
tenant selection,
target user type selection,
target entity lookup / selection,
coin quantity entry,
issuance action,
wallet snapshot for selected user,
and role-aware wallet operations (Company coin request approvals, Professional coin requests in tenant wallet routes).
Before assigning coins, the SuperAdmin shall be able to select the target tenant.

After selecting the tenant, the SuperAdmin shall be able to choose the type of recipient:
Company / Tenant
Coach / Professional
Individual

Once the target type is selected, the system shall present valid entities from that tenant for selection, except in the case of a Company-level allocation where the tenant itself may be the target entity.
The SuperAdmin shall be able to assign a positive integer number of coins to the selected target entity.
When coins are assigned to a user-level target, the logged-in recipient shall be able to see those coins in Manage Wallet / My Wallet.

Manage Wallet / My Wallet shall show at minimum:
total issued coins,
total utilized coins,
available / unused coins,
and wallet transaction history including debit reasons where applicable.

Every coin assignment shall create a ledger transaction record with metadata such as:
issuer,
tenant,
target type,
target entity,
quantity,
timestamp,
and reason / note where applicable.
The system shall separately track utilization events so that issued and used totals are not conflated.

Available coin balance shall be calculated as:

Available Coins = Total Issued Coins - Total Utilized Coins - Other Valid Debits + Valid Credits

For MVP, the primary calculation may be simplified to:

Available Coins = Total Issued Coins - Total Utilized Coins
The system shall preserve auditable wallet transactions and shall not depend only on a mutable current balance field.

Only the SuperAdmin shall be able to issue coins in this version of the Epic.

---

The expected SuperAdmin flow is:

1. Open SuperAdmin dashboard.
2. View wallet summary tile showing utilized / issued count.
3. Click tile or open Manage Coins from navigation.
4. Select tenant.
5. Select target user type.
6. Select specific target entity.
7. Enter coin quantity.
8. Submit assignment.
9. View confirmation and updated ledger / summary.

The expected recipient flow is:

1. Log in to StudioVerse.
2. Open My Wallet.
3. See current available coins.
4. Review issued and utilized values.
5. Review recent wallet history where applicable.

id

ownerType

totalIssuedCoins
totalUtilizedCoins
availableCoins
status
createdAt
updatedAt

The Wallet Transaction entity shall support at minimum:

id
walletId
tenantId
transactionType
creditOrDebit
coinAmount
issuedBy
issuedByRole
targetType
targetEntityId
targetEntityName
reason
referenceType
referenceId
balanceBefore
balanceAfter
status
createdAt

totalIssuedCoins
totalUtilizedCoins
totalUnusedCoins
tenantCountCovered
updatedAt

The system should support transaction types such as:

issue
adjustment_credit
adjustment_debit
utilization
reversal
expiry

Even if only issue and utilization are active in the first version, the schema should be future-ready.

Only SuperAdmin may assign coins in this release.
Coin assignment must be tenant-aware.
A target recipient must belong to the selected tenant.
Coin quantity must be a positive whole number.
Coin assignment must create a ledger transaction.
My Wallet must reflect valid issued balance for the logged-in owner.
Utilized coins must be tracked separately from issued coins.
Unused coins must equal issued coins minus utilized coins, unless additional debit rules are later introduced.
Dashboard summary must aggregate totals across all tenants.
Clicking the wallet summary tile must open the Manage Wallet page.
The system must preserve auditability and should not rely only on overwriting one balance field.

---

The wallet system should be implemented using a ledger-first model rather than a balance-only model.

That means:
every allocation or usage event creates a transaction record,
balances may be stored as denormalized summary fields for performance,
but the source of truth remains the transaction history.

This is recommended because it improves:
auditability,
debugging,
reconciliation,
future reporting,
and extensibility for coin-based platform usage.

The platform should maintain:
a wallet entity for current summarized balance,
and a wallet transaction collection for immutable balance events.

---

The SuperAdmin dashboard must present a compact but meaningful summary of wallet state through a tile such as:

Utilized / Issued = 10 / 20

Interpretation:
10 = total utilized coins
20 = total issued coins across all tenants

The system should also be capable of deriving:
unused coins,
utilization rate,
and allocation coverage by tenant,
even if only the utilized/issued tile is shown in the first release.

text:

so that I can start using wallet-based flows immediately after onboarding.

Acceptance Criteria

sectionTitle: Professional Can Request Coins from Associated Company (Completed)
text:
As a Professional,

so that I can request additional coins for platform usage without requiring SuperAdmin coordination.

Acceptance Criteria

Professional can enter a quantity and submit a request.

sectionTitle: Company Can Approve or Deny Coin Requests with Ruleset Enforcement (Completed)
text:
As a Company user, 
I want to view, approve, and deny pending coin requests from associated Professionals, 
so that I can govern coin distribution to team members responsibly.

Acceptance Criteria

Approve action transitions request status to "approved" and adds coins to Professional's wallet via ledger transaction.
Deny action transitions request status to "denied" with optional reason field.
Firestore rules restrict approve/deny to Company users only (isCompanyUser() constraint).
Badge count refreshes in real-time after action is taken.
Delete operations are permanently disabled (soft-delete pattern only).

End of Document

This document defines the business and functional requirements for the Manage Profile capability in StudioVerse.

The purpose of this Epic is to create a role-aware profile system that captures the minimum required information needed for users to participate meaningfully in the platform while allowing a stepped approach to profile enrichment over time.

This Epic also establishes the profile-completion gating behavior required before an Individual can be assigned an Assessment, Program, or Event, and lays the foundation for richer assessment quality, recruitment usage, search, matching, and future public profile scenarios such as Search Coach.

---

This document explains how Profile becomes a core platform object that supports onboarding, assignment readiness, better assessment quality, professional discovery, and future recruitment value creation.

This document defines profile scope, mandatory versus optional fields, role-specific profile sections, completion gating, dashboard prompting, and long-term extensibility for public and professional use cases.

This document provides the functional requirements needed to implement profile schemas, section-based update flows, completion-status evaluation, assignment eligibility checks, and role-dependent UI rendering.

This document provides the basis for validating profile completion, mandatory field enforcement, dashboard nudges, assignment gating, role-specific forms, editability rules, and future profile extensibility.

This document describes the expected experience for profile completion prompts, profile update pages, section-based forms, and informational guidance encouraging users to enrich optional data.

---

StudioVerse needs a structured Profile capability that supports the practical reality that users cannot be expected to complete a very large form all at once.

Therefore, the platform will follow a stepped profile completion approach. On first access to the dashboard, users who have not completed the mandatory part of their profile will be shown a top-level prompt asking them to complete their profile.

Selecting that prompt will take the user to an Update Profile page that contains a full profile layout with multiple sections. The mandatory section must be completed before an Individual becomes eligible to receive platform assignments such as Assessments, Programs, or Events.

All remaining sections may remain optional in the early stage, but the product will explicitly guide the user by stating that richer profile information can lead to better assessments and stronger platform outcomes.

The Profile object will support multiple role types:
Individual
Professional
Company

Professionals will have additional professional-facing fields, and in a future Epic such as Search Coach, that information can power a world-visible public coach profile.

Companies will also have their own basic profile structure including company identity and location data.

---

StudioVerse is a multi-tenant platform spanning coaching, training, and recruitment-oriented scenarios, which makes Profile more than a simple personal information form.

Profile data can support:
onboarding readiness,
assignment eligibility,
better contextual assessments,
professional and recruitment referrals,
future search and discovery,
tenant-aware data completeness,
and better decision-making across the platform.

Because the platform includes Individuals, Professionals, and Company entities, a one-size-fits-all profile form would be too rigid and too heavy. A role-aware and phased model is therefore required.

---

StudioVerse currently requires a formal Profile capability that:
captures the minimum mandatory data needed for platform participation,
supports role-aware extensions,
and avoids forcing users to fill everything in one go.

The platform also needs a clear mechanism to ensure that assignment-related experiences do not begin before the Individual has provided the minimum necessary profile information.

Without this capability:
users may enter core journeys with incomplete identity data,
assessments may lack useful context,
assignments may fail operationally,
profile quality will vary significantly,
and the platform will not have a reliable foundation for future search, recruitment, and public profile use cases.

---

The objectives of this Epic are:

Establish Profile as a canonical role-aware platform object.
Support profile creation and update for Individuals, Professionals, and Companies.
Define mandatory versus optional fields clearly.
Support a stepped profile completion experience instead of forcing full completion upfront.
Show a dashboard prompt requesting profile completion when mandatory data is missing.
Route users from the dashboard prompt to an Update Profile page.
Gate assignment eligibility for Individuals until mandatory profile fields are complete.
Support optional profile enrichment through structured sections.
Encourage optional enrichment by communicating that richer profiles lead to better assessments.
Support optional profile photo upload.
Lay groundwork for future public Professional profile visibility in Search Coach.

---

The following are out of scope for this Epic:

full public profile pages for search engines,
advanced social networking functionality,
recommendations engine logic,
professional ranking algorithms,
resume parsing,
external verification workflows,
public coach search listings,
and recruitment application pipelines.

These may be built in later epics, but this Epic focuses on profile capture, completion, gating, and update flows.

---

This Epic includes the following capabilities:

role-aware profile model,
mandatory and optional profile fields,
dashboard reminder / banner for incomplete mandatory profile,
Update Profile page with section-based layout,
optional profile picture upload,
assignment readiness gating for Individuals,
profile completion status evaluation,
Professional-specific fields,
Company-specific fields,
and future-ready schema support for public profile use cases.

---

This Epic does not include:

Search Coach public listing pages,
resume recommendation engines,
AI-written profile summaries,
public shareable profile URLs,
endorsements and ratings,
and company verification workflows.

---
A Profile is the structured identity and contextual information object associated with a platform actor such as an Individual, Professional, or Company.
Mandatory Profile Fields are the minimum set of fields that must be completed in order for the user to be considered profile-complete for core participation.
Optional Profile Fields are enrichment fields that are not required to activate basic eligibility but improve future platform outcomes.
Profile Completion is the state in which all mandatory fields for the relevant role have been provided and validated.
Profile Enrichment refers to the completion of additional non-mandatory sections that improve context, assessment quality, searchability, and future matching.
The Update Profile Page is the structured screen where the user reviews and completes profile information section by section.

The Assignment Eligibility Gate is the rule that prevents assignment of certain experiences, such as Assessments, Programs, or Events, until an Individual’s mandatory profile is complete.

---
The Individual must complete mandatory personal profile fields before being eligible for Assessments, Programs, or Events.
The Professional must also maintain mandatory identity information and may additionally complete professional-specific fields that will later support public profile visibility and search.
The Company profile must capture basic organization information and required location or identity data needed for platform usage.

The SuperAdmin may view profile completeness indirectly through downstream modules and can rely on profile readiness rules for assignment workflows.

---

The platform shall not require every possible profile field to be completed at once.

Instead, the platform shall:
require completion of a mandatory profile section first,
allow all other sections to remain optional,
and encourage users to enrich their profile progressively over time.

If a logged-in user has not completed the mandatory profile section, the dashboard shall show a visible top-level message asking the user to complete their profile.

Example intent:
“Please complete your profile to continue.”
“Complete your mandatory profile details before assignments can be enabled.”
Selecting the dashboard reminder shall take the user to the Update Profile page.

The Update Profile page shall communicate that while some fields are optional, completing them can improve platform outcomes such as better assessments.

---
The Individual Profile shall include personal identity, contact, location, education, experience, and enrichment fields relevant to assessments and future recruitment support.

The Professional Profile shall include all relevant mandatory identity fields plus professional-facing fields such as expertise, experience, professional summary, and future public-discovery-supporting fields.

The Company Profile shall include basic company information, address and location details, and other foundational organization fields needed for tenant-level identity.

---
